title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
A 34-year-old woman is in the trauma bay after a high-speed crash. Alert, oriented, full capacity. CT shows a grade IV splenic laceration; her hemoglobin has fallen from 13 to 7 in the last hour. The surgeon wants her in the OR, with pre-emptive transfusion. Tucked in her wallet is a signed, dated card declaring her a Jehovah’s Witness and refusing all blood products. Her parents have just arrived and are pleading with the team to “do whatever it takes.”
You are the nurse at her bedside.

What questions would you want answered before doing anything?
Who exactly is asking you to do what?
What do you think the right move is — and why?











Note



The legal frame. U.S. courts have, in nearly every case involving a competent adult, upheld the patient’s right to refuse blood — even when refusal is fatal. The harder cases involve minors, pregnancy, and unclear capacity. We will come back to all three later in the course.



Let students sit with this. We’ll come back to it after we have vocabulary. The whole rest of the lecture is, in a sense, equipping us to answer this question more carefully than “follow your gut.”





By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Define bioethics and explain why it emerged as a distinct field
Distinguish ethical claims from legal, religious, and personal-preference claims
Name and apply the four principles of biomedical ethics
Identify the premises and conclusion of a moral argument written in ordinary prose
Recognize a few common fallacies in bioethical reasoning

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.1 A Working Definition
Bioethics is the systematic study of ethical questions arising in:

The care of patients (clinical ethics)
Biomedical research on humans and animals (research ethics)
Public health policy and the allocation of scarce resources
New biotechnologies — genetics, AI, reproductive technology


“Systematic” matters. Bioethics isn’t just having opinions — it’s reasoning about them in a way others can examine, challenge, and refine.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.2 Why a Distinct Field?
A short list of mid-20th-century shocks:

The Nazi medical experiments exposed at Nuremberg (1945–1947)
The Tuskegee syphilis study (1932–1972), in which Black men were left untreated for decades
New technology that outran old norms: ventilators, dialysis, organ transplants, IVF
Patients and families demanding a voice the old paternalistic model didn’t give them

Bioethics is what grew up to answer the question: how should we govern this?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.3 Three Sample Questions


Clinical: Can a 16-year-old refuse chemotherapy her parents want her to have?
Research: Is it ethical to give half the participants a placebo when an effective drug already exists?
Policy: When ICU beds run out in a pandemic, who gets one?



Each of these is a recurring exam case. Same field, very different scales — bedside, study, society.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.1 Why the Distinctions Matter
Students often collapse these. They are not the same — and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes in bioethical reasoning.








Type of claim
Source of authority
Question it answers




Ethical
Reasons we can examine and share
What ought we to do?


Legal
Statutes, courts, regulators
What may or must we do under law?


Religious
Sacred texts, traditions, communities
What does this faith require?


Preference
Personal taste
What do I happen to want?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.2 Ethics vs. Law
The law is not a reliable guide to what is ethical.

Slavery in the United States was legal until 1865 — and was monstrously unethical the entire time.
The Tuskegee study was legal in 1932. It was wrong in 1932.
Conversely, jaywalking is illegal in most cities; almost no one thinks it is a moral failing.







Tip



A useful test for nurses: “Is it legal?” and “Is it the right thing for this patient?” are two different questions. Sometimes the law lags behind ethics; sometimes it overreaches.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.3 Ethics vs. Religion
Many of our patients — and many of you — draw moral guidance from religious tradition. That is appropriate. But:

A religious tradition can give its members reasons to act, but cannot, on its own, give non-members reasons to act.
Bioethical arguments need to work in a pluralistic society where patients, families, and clinicians come from many traditions and none.

We will treat religious commitments as important data about what patients value — not as conversation-stoppers in clinical reasoning.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.4 Ethics vs. Personal Preference

“I just don’t think it’s right.”

That is a feeling, not yet an argument. Ethical reasoning asks the next question: why? What reason, that another reasonable person could weigh, supports the conclusion?

This is the moment to flag that “I feel” / “I believe” are perfectly fine starting points — but bioethics asks us to take the next step.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.5 Thought Question

A nurse on the L&D floor is asked to assist with a second-trimester abortion for a patient whose fetus has a lethal anomaly. The procedure is legal; her own physician is performing it. The nurse declines on religious grounds and asks to be reassigned. Her hospital has a conscience clause — a policy permitting clinicians to opt out of specific procedures — and she has invoked it formally.

Is what she is doing legal? Ethical? Religiously required? A personal preference?
Could the answers come apart? In what scenario?
Now imagine the same refusal at 3 a.m. at a small rural hospital with no other nurse on shift. Anything change?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Where the duty actually lands. Most U.S. conscience-clause laws place the duty to still care for the patient on the institution, not on the individual clinician. That shifts the question from “can she refuse?” to “what does the institution owe the patient when she does?”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.1 A Brief Origin Story
In the wake of Tuskegee, a U.S. national commission produced the Belmont Report (1979) (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979). That same year, philosophers Tom Beauchamp and James Childress published Principles of Biomedical Ethics (Beauchamp and Childress 2019), proposing four principles meant to be:

Shared across ethical traditions
Specific enough to guide clinical decisions
General enough to apply to new technologies

The result is what’s often called principlism — and it remains the lingua franca of hospital ethics consults (Jonsen 1998).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.2 1. Autonomy
Autonomy — self-rule. Respect the patient’s right to make informed decisions about her own body and care.
In practice, autonomy underwrites:

Informed consent
The right to refuse treatment (even life-saving treatment)
Truth-telling and honest disclosure
Confidentiality


The Jehovah’s Witness case from the start of class is, at its core, an autonomy case. Hold that thought.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.3 Autonomy in Practice

Autonomy self-rule


The case. A 28-year-old with type-1 diabetes (A1c 11.2) declines an insulin pump for the third time. She is tired of being told what to do.
What respecting autonomy looks like here:

Make sure she understands the risks — disclosure
Assess decision-making capacity (she has it)
Document clearly; continue to offer support without coercion

Autonomy is the floor. It does not require us to abandon her — only not to override her.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.4 2. Beneficence
Beneficence — do good. Act in the patient’s best interest; promote welfare.
This sounds obvious until you ask: whose account of the patient’s good?

The clinician’s professional judgment?
The patient’s own values?
The family’s hopes?

Beneficence and autonomy can pull in opposite directions.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.5 Beneficence in Practice

Beneficence do good


The case. An 82-year-old with end-stage heart failure, fifth admission this year. The team recommends comfort-focused care; he wants “everything done.” His daughter asks the team to “talk him out of it.”
Whose idea of his good?

The team’s clinical judgment about prognosis?
His own stated wishes, formed over a long life?
His daughter’s fear of losing him?

Beneficence is not the same as paternalism. It is the discipline of finding out what this person counts as a life worth living — and acting in service of that.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.6 3. Non-Maleficence
Non-maleficence — first, do no harm (primum non nocere).


The oldest principle in Western medicine, traceable to the Hippocratic tradition (5th c. BCE). It does not mean “never do anything risky.” It means: weigh the harms, and don’t expose patients to harm without proportionate benefit.
Every chemotherapy regimen, every surgery, every medication is a deliberate small harm in service of a larger good.



Hippocrates, engraving by Peter Paul Rubens. Public domain (Wikimedia Commons).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.7 Non-Maleficence in Practice

Non-Maleficence do no harm


The case. A new chemotherapy regimen offers a 30% chance of remission and a 5% chance of fatal cardiotoxicity. Standard care offers neither.
The principle does not say “never harm.” It asks:

Is the expected harm proportionate to the expected benefit?
Does the patient understand both sides clearly enough to choose?

Every IV start is a small puncture wound. Every medication has side effects. The principle’s job is to forbid hidden, gratuitous, or disproportionate risk — not all risk.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.8 4. Justice
Justice — fair distribution of benefits and burdens.
In bioethics, justice shows up in three main forms:

Distributive justice — who gets the kidney, the ICU bed, the new drug?
Procedural justice — was the process fair, even if the outcome is hard?
Social justice — are we honest about the historical and structural causes of health disparities?


We’ll spend an entire lecture (L8) on justice. For now, just plant the flag: bioethics is not only about individual encounters.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.9 Justice in Practice

Justice treat fairly


The case. Two 55-year-olds arrive in the ED with chest pain and no obvious red flags. One is on Medicaid; the other has private insurance. Decades of research show that, on average, they will not get the same workup.
Justice at the bedside is rarely a kidney allocation decision. It looks like:

Are we ordering tests for clinical reasons — or for who the patient appears to be?
Are our floors and ICUs staffed and supplied equitably?
Where do we, as nurses, push back against disparate care?

Justice asks us to look up from the individual patient and notice the pattern.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.10 The Four Together





flowchart LR
  A[Autonomy<br/>respect choices] --- B[Beneficence<br/>do good]
  B --- N[Non-maleficence<br/>do no harm]
  N --- J[Justice<br/>treat fairly]
  J --- A
  style A fill:#cfe9ec,stroke:#0e7c86,stroke-width:2px
  style B fill:#fce9d6,stroke:#b07d1a,stroke-width:2px
  style N fill:#fad7d2,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
  style J fill:#dfe6ee,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px






A framework, not an algorithm. The principles tell you what to weigh — not, by themselves, how to weigh it.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.11 When Principles Conflict
Most interesting bioethics cases are conflicts between principles, not conflicts between good and evil.

Autonomy vs. Beneficence — the Jehovah’s Witness refusing transfusion
Beneficence vs. Non-Maleficence — risky surgery with a small chance of cure
Autonomy vs. Justice — vaccine refusal during a pandemic
Beneficence vs. Justice — spending heroic resources on one patient when others wait

The four principles do not resolve these tensions. They make them visible so we can argue about them carefully.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.12 Thought Question

Return to our trauma-bay patient — full capacity, signed refusal, parents at the door. Now you have vocabulary for what was hard.

Which principles point toward transfusing her? Which point away?
Which counts as the “harm” — letting her die, or doing something to her body she has explicitly refused?
Which principle would you weight most heavily, and why?
What single fact, if changed, would change your answer?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.13 After You’ve Discussed






Note



How the principles typically map.

The case is often framed as Beneficence and Non-Maleficence pulling toward transfusion, Autonomy pulling against.
But notice the twist: respecting her refusal may itself be the most beneficent act, and transfusing a competent patient against her will is itself a form of harm.
The principles do not decide the case. They give us shared language to argue about it.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.1 What Is an Argument?
An argument, in philosophy, is not a quarrel. It is a structured piece of reasoning:

One or more premises (claims offered as support)
A conclusion (the claim the premises are meant to support)

The job of the careful reader: find the conclusion first, then the premises, then ask whether the premises are true and whether they actually support the conclusion.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.2 Worked Example
A short argument you might hear on the floor:

“We have to transfuse her — she’ll die otherwise, and our job is to save lives.”








argument


P1

Premise 1
Without a transfusion,
the patient will die.



C

∴  Conclusion
We should transfuse her.



P1->C





P2

Premise 2
Our job is
to save lives.



P2->C

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.3 Hidden Assumptions
Sounds airtight — until you notice what’s missing. That argument quietly assumes:

“Saving lives” always outweighs the patient’s other wishes.
The patient’s refusal can be set aside because it’s “irrational.”
A nurse’s professional duty is identical to “extending biological life.”

Each of those is contestable. Surfacing hidden assumptions is half the work of bioethics.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.4 What Is a Fallacy?
A fallacy is an error in reasoning — an argument that looks like good support but isn’t.

Not the same as a lie. The speaker may sincerely believe it.
A fallacious argument can still have a true conclusion — the defect is in the structure, not the conclusion.
Most bioethics fallacies are informal: the form looks fine, but the content misleads through irrelevance, ambiguity, or hidden assumption.

The next two slides cover the six you will see most often.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.5 Common Fallacies in Bioethics (1/2)


Appeal to Nature Inferring that something is good (or bad) merely because it is natural (or unnatural). “IVF lets older women conceive — but it’s unnatural, so it must be wrong.”


Slippery Slope Predicting that allowing X will inevitably lead to a much worse Y, without showing how the slide actually happens. “If we permit medical aid in dying for the terminally ill, we’ll end up euthanizing the disabled.”


Ad Hominem Attacking the person making the argument rather than the argument itself. “You can’t take Peter Singer seriously on animal ethics — he holds a lot of unpopular views.”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.6 Common Fallacies in Bioethics (2/2)


Naturalistic Fallacy Inferring an ought directly from an is — that what happens in nature shows what should happen morally. “Humans evolved as omnivores, therefore eating meat is morally permissible.”


False Dichotomy Framing a complex issue as if there were only two options, when others exist. “Either we let the market ration health care, or the system will collapse.”


Appeal to Authority Treating the testimony of an authority as decisive even when they speak outside their expertise — or when other experts disagree. “My favorite podcaster says vaccines cause autism, and he has done a lot of research.”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.7 Practice — Spot the Fallacy

Identify the fallacy in each:

“Gene editing is wrong — it’s playing God.”
“If we let doctors help patients die, we’ll end up euthanizing the disabled.”
“You can’t trust her opinion on vaccines — she’s not even a real scientist.”
“Either we ration care by ability to pay, or the system collapses.”



Answers (in order): appeal to nature, slippery slope, ad hominem, false dichotomy.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.8 Wrapping Up Part A

Bioethics is the systematic study of moral questions in medicine, research, and policy. It is not the same as law, religion, or personal preference. The four principles — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice — give us a shared vocabulary for the conflicts we’ll see all semester. And every claim in this course will be evaluated as an argument: premises, conclusion, hidden assumptions, possible fallacies.
In Part B, we ask the next question: where do these principles come from? We meet the major ethical theories — utilitarian, Kantian, virtue, and care — that ground and sometimes contest the four principles.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.9 References


Beauchamp, Tom L., and James F. Childress. 2019. Principles of Biomedical Ethics. 8th ed. Oxford University Press.


Jonsen, Albert R. 1998. The Birth of Bioethics. Oxford University Press.


National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research. 1979. The Belmont Report: Ethical Principles and Guidelines for the Protection of Human Subjects of Research. Department of Health, Education,; Welfare.

Every IV start is a small puncture wound. Every medication has side effects. The principle’s job is to forbid hidden, gratuitous, or disproportionate risk — not all risk.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4. Justice
Justice — fair distribution of benefits and burdens.
In bioethics, justice shows up in three main forms:

Distributive justice — who gets the kidney, the ICU bed, the new drug?
Procedural justice — was the process fair, even if the outcome is hard?
Social justice — are we honest about the historical and structural causes of health disparities?


We’ll spend an entire lecture (L8) on justice. For now, just plant the flag: bioethics is not only about individual encounters.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Justice in Practice

Justice treat fairly


The case. Two 55-year-olds arrive in the ED with chest pain and no obvious red flags. One is on Medicaid; the other has private insurance. Decades of research show that, on average, they will not get the same workup.
Justice at the bedside is rarely a kidney allocation decision. It looks like:

Are we ordering tests for clinical reasons — or for who the patient appears to be?
Are our floors and ICUs staffed and supplied equitably?
Where do we, as nurses, push back against disparate care?

Justice asks us to look up from the individual patient and notice the pattern.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
The Four Together





flowchart LR
  A[Autonomy<br/>respect choices] --- B[Beneficence<br/>do good]
  B --- N[Non-maleficence<br/>do no harm]
  N --- J[Justice<br/>treat fairly]
  J --- A
  style A fill:#cfe9ec,stroke:#0e7c86,stroke-width:2px
  style B fill:#fce9d6,stroke:#b07d1a,stroke-width:2px
  style N fill:#fad7d2,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
  style J fill:#dfe6ee,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px






A framework, not an algorithm. The principles tell you what to weigh — not, by themselves, how to weigh it.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
When Principles Conflict
Most interesting bioethics cases are conflicts between principles, not conflicts between good and evil.

Autonomy vs. Beneficence — the Jehovah’s Witness refusing transfusion
Beneficence vs. Non-Maleficence — risky surgery with a small chance of cure
Autonomy vs. Justice — vaccine refusal during a pandemic
Beneficence vs. Justice — spending heroic resources on one patient when others wait

The four principles do not resolve these tensions. They make them visible so we can argue about them carefully.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Thought Question

Return to our trauma-bay patient — full capacity, signed refusal, parents at the door. Now you have vocabulary for what was hard.

Which principles point toward transfusing her? Which point away?
Which counts as the “harm” — letting her die, or doing something to her body she has explicitly refused?
Which principle would you weight most heavily, and why?
What single fact, if changed, would change your answer?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


How the principles typically map.

The case is often framed as Beneficence and Non-Maleficence pulling toward transfusion, Autonomy pulling against.
But notice the twist: respecting her refusal may itself be the most beneficent act, and transfusing a competent patient against her will is itself a form of harm.
The principles do not decide the case. They give us shared language to argue about it.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
What Is an Argument?
An argument, in philosophy, is not a quarrel. It is a structured piece of reasoning:

One or more premises (claims offered as support)
A conclusion (the claim the premises are meant to support)

The job of the careful reader: find the conclusion first, then the premises, then ask whether the premises are true and whether they actually support the conclusion.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Worked Example
A short argument you might hear on the floor:

“We have to transfuse her — she’ll die otherwise, and our job is to save lives.”








argument


P1

Premise 1
Without a transfusion,
the patient will die.



C

∴  Conclusion
We should transfuse her.



P1->C





P2

Premise 2
Our job is
to save lives.



P2->C

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Hidden Assumptions
Sounds airtight — until you notice what’s missing. That argument quietly assumes:

“Saving lives” always outweighs the patient’s other wishes.
The patient’s refusal can be set aside because it’s “irrational.”
A nurse’s professional duty is identical to “extending biological life.”

Each of those is contestable. Surfacing hidden assumptions is half the work of bioethics.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
What Is a Fallacy?
A fallacy is an error in reasoning — an argument that looks like good support but isn’t.

Not the same as a lie. The speaker may sincerely believe it.
A fallacious argument can still have a true conclusion — the defect is in the structure, not the conclusion.
Most bioethics fallacies are informal: the form looks fine, but the content misleads through irrelevance, ambiguity, or hidden assumption.

The next two slides cover the six you will see most often.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Common Fallacies in Bioethics (1/2)


Appeal to Nature Inferring that something is good (or bad) merely because it is natural (or unnatural). “IVF lets older women conceive — but it’s unnatural, so it must be wrong.”


Slippery Slope Predicting that allowing X will inevitably lead to a much worse Y, without showing how the slide actually happens. “If we permit medical aid in dying for the terminally ill, we’ll end up euthanizing the disabled.”


Ad Hominem Attacking the person making the argument rather than the argument itself. “You can’t take Peter Singer seriously on animal ethics — he holds a lot of unpopular views.”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Common Fallacies in Bioethics (2/2)


Naturalistic Fallacy Inferring an ought directly from an is — that what happens in nature shows what should happen morally. “Humans evolved as omnivores, therefore eating meat is morally permissible.”


False Dichotomy Framing a complex issue as if there were only two options, when others exist. “Either we let the market ration health care, or the system will collapse.”


Appeal to Authority Treating the testimony of an authority as decisive even when they speak outside their expertise — or when other experts disagree. “My favorite podcaster says vaccines cause autism, and he has done a lot of research.”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Practice — Spot the Fallacy

Identify the fallacy in each:

“Gene editing is wrong — it’s playing God.”
“If we let doctors help patients die, we’ll end up euthanizing the disabled.”
“You can’t trust her opinion on vaccines — she’s not even a real scientist.”
“Either we ration care by ability to pay, or the system collapses.”



Answers (in order): appeal to nature, slippery slope, ad hominem, false dichotomy.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Wrapping Up Part A

Bioethics is the systematic study of moral questions in medicine, research, and policy. It is not the same as law, religion, or personal preference. The four principles — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice — give us a shared vocabulary for the conflicts we’ll see all semester. And every claim in this course will be evaluated as an argument: premises, conclusion, hidden assumptions, possible fallacies.
In Part B, we ask the next question: where do these principles come from? We meet the major ethical theories — utilitarian, Kantian, virtue, and care — that ground and sometimes contest the four principles.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
References


Beauchamp, Tom L., and James F. Childress. 2019. Principles of Biomedical Ethics. 8th ed. Oxford University Press.


Jonsen, Albert R. 1998. The Birth of Bioethics. Oxford University Press.


National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research. 1979. The Belmont Report: Ethical Principles and Guidelines for the Protection of Human Subjects of Research. Department of Health, Education,; Welfare.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Imagine you are a physician in the mid-20th century, asked to participate in a study monitoring a known, potentially fatal disease without offering the patients treatment, even after an effective cure is discovered. The rationale provided to you is that science needs to observe the natural progression of the illness to better understand it for future generations. The patients, largely from marginalized communities, are told only that they are receiving “special free care.” At the time, your profession operates largely on a culture of paternalism—“doctor knows best”—with very few formal guidelines on human experimentation. How do you respond? Where do you look for moral guidance when the law and standard practices of your day seem insufficient?




By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Analyze the historical shift from paternalistic medical models to autonomy-based models.
Identify the core principles introduced by the Nuremberg Code and how they revolutionized medical research.
Explain the ethical failings of the Tuskegee Syphilis Study and its impact on modern bioethics.
Compare foundational bioethics documents, including the Declaration of Helsinki and the Belmont Report.
Understand the origin and role of Institutional Review Boards (IRBs) and ethics committees.





When people think of medical ethics, they often start with ancient Greece. The Hippocratic tradition is often summarized in the public imagination by a single phrase: “first, do no harm” (often cited in Latin as primum non nocere). However, this specific phrase does not actually appear in the original Hippocratic Oath, but rather in another Hippocratic text called Of the Epidemics.
What the original oath did say was quite profound for its time. It established a covenant between the physician and their teachers, as well as a commitment to act for the benefit of the sick and keep them from harm or injustice. It also laid foundational rules for confidentiality, a principle that remains a cornerstone of the medical profession today.






Figure 1: Hippocrates of Kos. Engraving by Peter Paul Rubens, 1638. Wikimedia Commons, public domain.





The Hippocratic Oath was revolutionary because it separated medicine from strict religious or mystical practices, framing it instead as a moral profession governed by defined duties. The core duties outlined included:

Beneficence: Acting in the patient’s best interest.
Non-maleficence: Avoiding harm or injustice.
Confidentiality: Keeping patient information private.
Professionalism: Maintaining the integrity of the teaching and the profession.




However, the Hippocratic tradition also carried distinct limitations. It was fundamentally a paternalistic document. It assumed that the physician possessed the knowledge and authority to decide what was best for the patient, with little to no emphasis placed on the patient’s own consent or autonomy. For centuries, “doctor knows best” was not just a colloquialism; it was the prevailing ethical standard. In this view, a patient was more like a child needing guidance, and the doctor was the wise parent.

Medical Paternalism
Paternalism in medicine refers to the practice of physicians making decisions for adult patients without their consent, ostensibly for the patients’ own good. Similar to how a parent might make decisions for a child, paternalistic medicine assumes the patient lacks the expertise to choose wisely. While often well-intentioned, this model severely limits personal autonomy.





Leading up to the 20th century, the paternalistic model remained the dominant ethical framework. Medicine was advancing rapidly—the discovery of germs, the advent of reliable anesthesia, and structural changes to medical education meant that doctors were becoming increasingly effective, and increasingly powerful.
However, as medicine and biology advanced, new and dangerous ideologies began to emerge, often cloaked in the language of science and public health. Because the existing ethical framework heavily favored the authority of the physician and the “greater good” over individual patient autonomy, it left patients highly vulnerable to abuse in the name of scientific progress.


One of the most significant and tragic developments was the rise of the eugenics movement, which gained substantial traction in both the United States and Europe during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Proponents of eugenics believed that the human race could be “improved” by encouraging the reproduction of those deemed “fit” and actively preventing the reproduction of those labeled “unfit” (often marginalized populations, the poor, and people with disabilities).
This ideology led to widespread human rights violations, including forced sterilization laws enacted in numerous US states, which disproportionately affected women of color and individuals in state institutions.

If the stated goal of public health is to improve the well-being of the population as a whole, what happens when that goal directly conflicts with the reproductive rights and bodily autonomy of individuals? E.g., the infamous Buck v. Bell (1927) Supreme Court decision.





The darkest consequences of eugenics and unchecked medical authority culminated in the atrocities committed by Nazi doctors during World War II. Under the guise of medical research, horrific and fatal experiments were conducted on concentration camp prisoners without their consent.
These included: - Freezing experiments: To simulate the conditions of pilots transferring over cold water, prisoners were submerged in ice water and observed until death. - High-altitude tests: Prisoners were placed in decompression chambers to test the limits of human endurance, often resulting in agonizing deaths. - Infection studies: Healthy individuals were intentionally infected with malaria, typhus, and tuberculosis to test experimental drugs.
The medical establishment in Nazi Germany largely capitulated to, and actively participated in, the regime’s ideology. Doctors became agents of the state rather than advocates for the patient.



Following the defeat of Nazi Germany, the world was forced to confront these medical atrocities during the “Doctors’ Trial” in Nuremberg. Twenty-three medical professionals and administrators stood trial for war crimes and crimes against humanity.






Figure 2: The Nuremberg Doctors’ Trial. US National Archives. Wikimedia Commons, public domain.



The court was faced with a significant problem: there was no internationally accepted standard or code of ethics governing human experimentation that they could definitively point to as having been violated. The Nazi doctors defended themselves by pointing out that prior to the war, there was no international law explicitly forbidding their actions, and they argued that their experiments were for the greater good of their nation’s war effort.



To address this gap and provide a legal and ethical framework for their judgments, the judges formulated the Nuremberg Code (Nuremberg Military Tribunals 1947). This groundbreaking document established ten rules for permissible medical experiments.


The most revolutionary aspect of the Code was its absolute first principle:

“The voluntary consent of the human subject is absolutely essential. This means that the person involved should have legal capacity to give consent; should be so situated as to be able to exercise free power of choice…”

This marked a seismic shift in medical ethics. It explicitly stated that the individual’s right to choose—their autonomy—superseded the interests of science or society.



In addition to voluntary consent, the Code mandated: - The experiment should yield fruitful results for the good of society, unprocurable by other methods. - The experiment should be based on prior animal knowledge. - Avoid unnecessary physical and mental suffering and injury. - No experiment should be conducted where there is an a priori reason to believe death or disabling injury will occur. - The degree of risk should never exceed the humanitarian importance of the problem. - Proper preparations must be made to protect the experimental subject against even remote possibilities of injury. - The experiment should be conducted only by scientifically qualified persons. - The subject should be at liberty to bring the experiment to an end at any time. - The scientist must be prepared to terminate the experiment at any stage if they believe it is likely to result in injury or death.

The Nuremberg Code was drafted specifically in response to wartime atrocities. If you were a physician in a peaceful democracy in 1950, might you feel that these stringent guidelines didn’t apply to “normal” medical research?





While the Nuremberg Code set an international standard, abuses in research were not confined to wartime or totalitarian regimes. One of the most infamous ethical breaches in American medical history occurred during peacetime, overlapping with and extending decades past the Nuremberg trials: the Tuskegee Study of Untreated Syphilis in the Negro Male (Brandt 1978).
Initiated in 1932 by the U.S. Public Health Service, the study enrolled 600 Black men from impoverished rural areas in Alabama, 399 of whom had latent syphilis. The researchers wanted to observe the natural progression of the disease.


The men were not told they had syphilis. Instead, they were told they were being treated for “bad blood,” a local colloquialism for various ailments. In exchange for participating, they received free medical exams, meals, and burial insurance. Because these men were extremely poor and largely lacked access to regular medical care, these incentives were highly coercive.






Figure 3: Doctor drawing blood from a participant in the Tuskegee Syphilis Study. CDC Archives. Wikimedia Commons, public domain.






The most egregious ethical failure occurred in the 1940s when penicillin became the recognized, widely available, and highly effective cure for syphilis.
Instead of treating the men and ending the study, the researchers deliberately withheld this information and the treatment. They wanted to see the study through to autopsy. They actively intervened to prevent the men from getting care elsewhere, even contacting local draft boards to prevent the men from receiving standard medical treatments when drafted for WWII.
The study continued for 40 years, only stopping in 1972 after a whistleblower, Peter Buxtun, leaked the story to the press, sparking public outrage. Dozens of men died from untreated syphilis, wives were infected, and children were born with congenital syphilis.



The revelation of Tuskegee sent shockwaves through the American public. It completely destroyed trust in medical institutions among many minority communities—a legacy of mistrust that persists and impacts public health efforts today. It made it undeniably clear that the US needed strict, legally enforced regulations to protect human research subjects. Reliance on the good intentions of professional researchers was no longer acceptable.





Where does the word ‘bioethics’ come from?
The term was coined in 1970 by Van Rensselaer Potter, a biochemist and oncology researcher. He envisioned bioethics as a “science of survival,” a bridge between the biological sciences and humanistic values, necessary to navigate the complexities of a rapidly advancing technological world (Potter 1970). Around the same time, the Kennedy Institute of Ethics and the Hastings Center were founded.

The public outcry following the Tuskegee revelations sparked a demand for formal mechanisms of accountability. It led directly to the National Research Act of 1974.
This act created the National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research. Their task was immense: to identify the basic ethical principles that should definitively underlie the conduct of biomedical research involving human subjects.



The result of the Commission’s work was the publication of the Belmont Report in 1979 (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979). The report was not a set of strict rules, but rather a framework of foundational principles intended to help resolve ethical problems.
The report identified three core principles:


This principle requires acknowledging the autonomy of individuals. People should be treated as autonomous agents, and persons with diminished autonomy (e.g., children, prisoners) are entitled to protection. This principle is the ethical foundation for the requirement of informed consent in both research and clinical care.



Following the Hippocratic tradition, this requires making efforts to secure the well-being of the patient or subject. In the Belmont context, it is formalized as two general rules: a. Do no harm. b. Maximize possible benefits and minimize possible harms.



This principle demands fairness in the distribution of the burdens and benefits of research. The Tuskegee study was a profound violation of justice—a vulnerable population carried the massive burden of the research, while they were intentionally excluded from any benefits (the cure).
The Belmont Report, along with Beauchamp and Childress’s influential textbook Principles of Biomedical Ethics (published in the same year) (Beauchamp and Childress 2019), solidified the framework of “principlism” that dominates Western bioethics today.




The transition from unquestioned medical paternalism to modern, regulated bioethics did not happen overnight. It was catalyzed by crisis and tragedy.





timeline
    title Bioethics Milestones (1900-2000)
    section Early 20th C.
        1932 : Tuskegee Syphilis Study begins
    section Mid 20th C.
        1945 : Nuremberg Doctors' Trial
        1947 : The Nuremberg Code established
        1954 : First successful kidney transplant
        1964 : Declaration of Helsinki adopted
    section Late 20th C.
        1970 : The term 'Bioethics' is coined
        1972 : Tuskegee Study exposed & ended
        1974 : National Research Act passed
        1979 : The Belmont Report published
        1990 : Human Genome Project begins









While they share common historical momentum, the major documents that shaped modern bioethics have slightly different focuses, intended audiences, and origins. We can compare the three most influential pieces.









Feature
The Nuremberg Code (1947)
Declaration of Helsinki (1964)
The Belmont Report (1979)




Origin / Context
War Crimes Tribunal, post-WWII. Created by judges.
World Medical Association. Created by physicians.
US National Commission, post-Tuskegee. Created by an ethics panel.


Primary Audience
Legal and international bodies, the broader world.
Physicians and the global medical community.
US Researchers, IRBs, and policymakers.


Core Focus
Absolute necessity of voluntary, informed consent.
Ethical principles for medical research specifically by physicians.
Foundational principles: Respect for Persons, Beneficence, Justice.


Enduring Legacy
The first, uncompromising international standard for human subjects.
A living document, regularly updated (latest 2013) that guides global physician-researchers (World Medical Association 1964).
Exists as the primary theoretical basis for US federal research regulations (The Common Rule).






The legacy of these historical events is embedded directly in how medical research and clinical care are governed today. The abuses of the past demonstrated that individual researchers or doctors cannot always be trusted to objectively balance scientific goals with human rights due to conflicting interests.
This realization mandated structural oversight, leading to the creation of Institutional Review Boards (IRBs) for research and Clinical Ethics Committees in hospitals.


In the United States, an IRB is a specialized committee empowered to approve, monitor, and review biomedical and behavioral research involving humans.

Purpose: Their primary purpose is to ensure the rights and welfare of human subjects are protected, largely utilizing the Belmont Report’s framework.
Requirement: Any research institution (university, hospital) receiving federal funding must maintain an IRB, and all human subject research must be approved prior to starting.
Composition: They must be diverse, including scientists, non-scientists, and members of the community unaffiliated with the institution.




While IRBs govern research, clinical ethics committees operate within hospitals to assist with difficult situations in active patient care.

Function: They serve as an advisory body when clinicians, patients, and families disagree or face moral dilemmas regarding a treatment plan.
Focus: They often deliberate on issues of life-sustaining treatment, decision-making capacity, surrogate decision-making, and resource allocation.
Goal: To facilitate communication and provide a structured, multidisciplinary perspective (involving doctors, nurses, social workers, chaplains, and ethicists) to resolve complex moral disagreements.

These bodies exist because history taught us that ethical oversight must be formal, interdisciplinary, independent, and completely transparent.





Medical ethics evolved dramatically from the paternalistic Hippocratic tradition to a modern framework centered on patient autonomy and human rights. This monumental shift was catalyzed by horrific abuses—from the Nazi medical experiments to the Tuskegee Syphilis Study here in the US. These tragedies necessitated the creation of foundational texts like the Nuremberg Code and the Belmont Report, and the establishment of formal oversight bodies like IRBs, to ensure that the advancement of science never again supersedes the rights of the individual.

It made it undeniably clear that the US needed strict, legally enforced regulations to protect human research subjects. Reliance on the good intentions of professional researchers was no longer acceptable.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The legacy of these historical events is embedded directly in how medical research and clinical care are governed today. The abuses of the past demonstrated that individual researchers or doctors cannot always be trusted to objectively balance scientific goals with human rights due to conflicting interests.
This realization mandated structural oversight, leading to the creation of Institutional Review Boards (IRBs) for research and Clinical Ethics Committees in hospitals.


In the United States, an IRB is a specialized committee empowered to approve, monitor, and review biomedical and behavioral research involving humans.

Purpose: Their primary purpose is to ensure the rights and welfare of human subjects are protected, largely utilizing the Belmont Report’s framework.
Requirement: Any research institution (university, hospital) receiving federal funding must maintain an IRB, and all human subject research must be approved prior to starting.
Composition: They must be diverse, including scientists, non-scientists, and members of the community unaffiliated with the institution.




While IRBs govern research, clinical ethics committees operate within hospitals to assist with difficult situations in active patient care.

Function: They serve as an advisory body when clinicians, patients, and families disagree or face moral dilemmas regarding a treatment plan.
Focus: They often deliberate on issues of life-sustaining treatment, decision-making capacity, surrogate decision-making, and resource allocation.
Goal: To facilitate communication and provide a structured, multidisciplinary perspective (involving doctors, nurses, social workers, chaplains, and ethicists) to resolve complex moral disagreements.

These bodies exist because history taught us that ethical oversight must be formal, interdisciplinary, independent, and completely transparent.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text: Medical ethics evolved dramatically from the paternalistic Hippocratic tradition to a modern framework centered on patient autonomy and human rights. This monumental shift was catalyzed by horrific abuses—from the Nazi medical experiments to the Tuskegee Syphilis Study here in the US. title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: Opening: A Case to Sit With
text:
Opening: A Case to Sit With
Imagine you are a physician in the mid-20th century, asked to participate in a study monitoring a known, potentially fatal disease without offering the patients treatment, even after an effective cure is discovered. The rationale provided to you is that science needs to observe the natural progression of the illness to better understand it for future generations. The patients, largely from marginalized communities, are told only that they are receiving “special free care.” At the time, your profession operates largely on a culture of paternalism—“doctor knows best”—with very few formal guidelines on human experimentation. How do you respond? Where do you look for moral guidance when the law and standard practices of your day seem insufficient?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
What We’re Doing Today

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Analyze the historical shift from paternalistic medical models to autonomy-based models.
Identify the core principles introduced by the Nuremberg Code and how they revolutionized medical research.
Explain the ethical failings of the Tuskegee Syphilis Study and its impact on modern bioethics.
Compare foundational bioethics documents, including the Declaration of Helsinki and the Belmont Report.
Understand the origin and role of Institutional Review Boards (IRBs) and ethics committees.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Hippocratic Tradition
When people think of medical ethics, they often start with ancient Greece. The Hippocratic tradition is often summarized in the public imagination by a single phrase: “first, do no harm” (often cited in Latin as primum non nocere). However, this specific phrase does not actually appear in the original Hippocratic Oath, but rather in another Hippocratic text called Of the Epidemics.
What the original oath did say was quite profound for its time. It established a covenant between the physician and their teachers, as well as a commitment to act for the benefit of the sick and keep them from harm or injustice. It also laid foundational rules for confidentiality, a principle that remains a cornerstone of the medical profession today.


Figure 1: Hippocrates of Kos. Engraving by Peter Paul Rubens, 1638. Wikimedia Commons, public domain.
Principles in the Oath
The Hippocratic Oath was revolutionary because it separated medicine from strict religious or mystical practices, framing it instead as a moral profession governed by defined duties. The core duties outlined included:

Beneficence: Acting in the patient’s best interest.
Non-maleficence: Avoiding harm or injustice.
Confidentiality: Keeping patient information private.
Professionalism: Maintaining the integrity of the teaching and the profession.

The Problem of Paternalism
However, the Hippocratic tradition also carried distinct limitations. It was fundamentally a paternalistic document. It assumed that the physician possessed the knowledge and authority to decide what was best for the patient, with little to no emphasis placed on the patient’s own consent or autonomy. For centuries, “doctor knows best” was not just a colloquialism; it was the prevailing ethical standard. In this view, a patient was more like a child needing guidance, and the doctor was the wise parent.

Medical Paternalism
Paternalism in medicine refers to the practice of physicians making decisions for adult patients without their consent, ostensibly for the patients’ own good. Similar to how a parent might make decisions for a child, paternalistic medicine assumes the patient lacks the expertise to choose wisely. While often well-intentioned, this model severely limits personal autonomy.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Pre-WWII Medical Ethics
Leading up to the 20th century, the paternalistic model remained the dominant ethical framework. Medicine was advancing rapidly—the discovery of germs, the advent of reliable anesthesia, and structural changes to medical education meant that doctors were becoming increasingly effective, and increasingly powerful.
However, as medicine and biology advanced, new and dangerous ideologies began to emerge, often cloaked in the language of science and public health. Because the existing ethical framework heavily favored the authority of the physician and the “greater good” over individual patient autonomy, it left patients highly vulnerable to abuse in the name of scientific progress.
The Rise of Eugenics
One of the most significant and tragic developments was the rise of the eugenics movement, which gained substantial traction in both the United States and Europe during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Proponents of eugenics believed that the human race could be “improved” by encouraging the reproduction of those deemed “fit” and actively preventing the reproduction of those labeled “unfit” (often marginalized populations, the poor, and people with disabilities).
This ideology led to widespread human rights violations, including forced sterilization laws enacted in numerous US states, which disproportionately affected women of color and individuals in state institutions.

If the stated goal of public health is to improve the well-being of the population as a whole, what happens when that goal directly conflicts with the reproductive rights and bodily autonomy of individuals? E.g., the infamous Buck v. Bell (1927) Supreme Court decision.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Nazi Medical Experiments (1939–1945)
The darkest consequences of eugenics and unchecked medical authority culminated in the atrocities committed by Nazi doctors during World War II. Under the guise of medical research, horrific and fatal experiments were conducted on concentration camp prisoners without their consent.
These included: - Freezing experiments: To simulate the conditions of pilots transferring over cold water, prisoners were submerged in ice water and observed until death. - High-altitude tests: Prisoners were placed in decompression chambers to test the limits of human endurance, often resulting in agonizing deaths. - Infection studies: Healthy individuals were intentionally infected with malaria, typhus, and tuberculosis to test experimental drugs.
The medical establishment in Nazi Germany largely capitulated to, and actively participated in, the regime’s ideology. Doctors became agents of the state rather than advocates for the patient.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Doctors’ Trial (1946–1947)
Following the defeat of Nazi Germany, the world was forced to confront these medical atrocities during the “Doctors’ Trial” in Nuremberg. Twenty-three medical professionals and administrators stood trial for war crimes and crimes against humanity.


Figure 2: The Nuremberg Doctors’ Trial. US National Archives. Wikimedia Commons, public domain.
The court was faced with a significant problem: there was no internationally accepted standard or code of ethics governing human experimentation that they could definitively point to as having been violated. The Nazi doctors defended themselves by pointing out that prior to the war, there was no international law explicitly forbidding their actions, and they argued that their experiments were for the greater good of their nation’s war effort.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Nuremberg Code (1947)
To address this gap and provide a legal and ethical framework for their judgments, the judges formulated the Nuremberg Code (Nuremberg Military Tribunals 1947). This groundbreaking document established ten rules for permissible medical experiments.
The First Principle
The most revolutionary aspect of the Code was its absolute first principle:

“The voluntary consent of the human subject is absolutely essential. This means that the person involved should have legal capacity to give consent; should be so situated as to be able to exercise free power of choice…”

This marked a seismic shift in medical ethics. It explicitly stated that the individual’s right to choose—their autonomy—superseded the interests of science or society.
Other Key Principles of the Code
In addition to voluntary consent, the Code mandated: - The experiment should yield fruitful results for the good of society, unprocurable by other methods. - The experiment should be based on prior animal knowledge. - Avoid unnecessary physical and mental suffering and injury. - No experiment should be conducted where there is an a priori reason to believe death or disabling injury will occur. - The degree of risk should never exceed the humanitarian importance of the problem. - Proper preparations must be made to protect the experimental subject against even remote possibilities of injury. - The experiment should be conducted only by scientifically qualified persons. - The subject should be at liberty to bring the experiment to an end at any time. - The scientist must be prepared to terminate the experiment at any stage if they believe it is likely to result in injury or death.

The Nuremberg Code was drafted specifically in response to wartime atrocities. If you were a physician in a peaceful democracy in 1950, might you feel that these stringent guidelines didn’t apply to “normal” medical research?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Tuskegee Syphilis Study (1932–1972)
While the Nuremberg Code set an international standard, abuses in research were not confined to wartime or totalitarian regimes. One of the most infamous ethical breaches in American medical history occurred during peacetime, overlapping with and extending decades past the Nuremberg trials: the Tuskegee Study of Untreated Syphilis in the Negro Male (Brandt 1978).
Initiated in 1932 by the U.S. Public Health Service, the study enrolled 600 Black men from impoverished rural areas in Alabama, 399 of whom had latent syphilis. The researchers wanted to observe the natural progression of the disease.
Deception and Coercion
The men were not told they had syphilis. Instead, they were told they were being treated for “bad blood,” a local colloquialism for various ailments. In exchange for participating, they received free medical exams, meals, and burial insurance. Because these men were extremely poor and largely lacked access to regular medical care, these incentives were highly coercive.


Figure 3: Doctor drawing blood from a participant in the Tuskegee Syphilis Study. CDC Archives. Wikimedia Commons, public domain.
The Fundamental Ethical Breach
The most egregious ethical failure occurred in the 1940s when penicillin became the recognized, widely available, and highly effective cure for syphilis.
Instead of treating the men and ending the study, the researchers deliberately withheld this information and the treatment. They wanted to see the study through to autopsy. They actively intervened to prevent the men from getting care elsewhere, even contacting local draft boards to prevent the men from receiving standard medical treatments when drafted for WWII.
The study continued for 40 years, only stopping in 1972 after a whistleblower, Peter Buxtun, leaked the story to the press, sparking public outrage. Dozens of men died from untreated syphilis, wives were infected, and children were born with congenital syphilis.
The Aftermath
The revelation of Tuskegee sent shockwaves through the American public. It completely destroyed trust in medical institutions among many minority communities—a legacy of mistrust that persists and impacts public health efforts today. It made it undeniably clear that the US needed strict, legally enforced regulations to protect human research subjects. Reliance on the good intentions of professional researchers was no longer acceptable.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Rise of Bioethics

Where does the word ‘bioethics’ come from?
The term was coined in 1970 by Van Rensselaer Potter, a biochemist and oncology researcher. He envisioned bioethics as a “science of survival,” a bridge between the biological sciences and humanistic values, necessary to navigate the complexities of a rapidly advancing technological world (Potter 1970). Around the same time, the Kennedy Institute of Ethics and the Hastings Center were founded.

The public outcry following the Tuskegee revelations sparked a demand for formal mechanisms of accountability. It led directly to the National Research Act of 1974.
This act created the National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research. Their task was immense: to identify the basic ethical principles that should definitively underlie the conduct of biomedical research involving human subjects.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Belmont Report (1979)
The result of the Commission’s work was the publication of the Belmont Report in 1979 (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979). The report was not a set of strict rules, but rather a framework of foundational principles intended to help resolve ethical problems.
The report identified three core principles:
1. Respect for Persons
This principle requires acknowledging the autonomy of individuals. People should be treated as autonomous agents, and persons with diminished autonomy (e.g., children, prisoners) are entitled to protection. This principle is the ethical foundation for the requirement of informed consent in both research and clinical care.
2. Beneficence
Following the Hippocratic tradition, this requires making efforts to secure the well-being of the patient or subject. In the Belmont context, it is formalized as two general rules: a. Do no harm. b. Maximize possible benefits and minimize possible harms.
3. Justice
This principle demands fairness in the distribution of the burdens and benefits of research. The Tuskegee study was a profound violation of justice—a vulnerable population carried the massive burden of the research, while they were intentionally excluded from any benefits (the cure).
The Belmont Report, along with Beauchamp and Childress’s influential textbook Principles of Biomedical Ethics (published in the same year) (Beauchamp and Childress 2019), solidified the framework of “principlism” that dominates Western bioethics today.

Milestones in Bioethics
The transition from unquestioned medical paternalism to modern, regulated bioethics did not happen overnight. It was catalyzed by crisis and tragedy.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Bridge from Part A
In Part A we asked: what are the four principles, and how do you read a moral argument?
Part B asks the next question:

Where do the principles come from — and how do we weigh them when they conflict?

That is where ethical theory comes in.

By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

State the core commitment of utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, and care ethics
Explain how each theory grounds or prioritizes the four principles differently
Apply at least two theories to the same clinical case and show they yield different answers

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
What Theories Do
The four principles — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice — give us a shared vocabulary. But they do not, by themselves, tell us which principle to prioritize when they conflict (Beauchamp and Childress 2019).
Ethical theories fill that gap. Think of them as lenses:

Each focuses on something real
Each has blind spots
No single lens sees everything

We will use all four throughout the course — not to pick a winner, but to see more of the problem.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
A Roadmap








Theory

Remind students: these will appear in almost every subsequent lecture. Abortion, end of life, justice — each one sees these theories pulling differently.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Mill and Bentham




J. S. Mill, c. 1870. London Stereoscopic Co. Public domain.




Jeremy Bentham, by H. W. Pickersgill. Public domain.



“Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness; wrong as they tend to produce the reverse of happiness.” — Mill (Mill 1863)

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
The Greatest Happiness Principle
Utilitarianism judges actions entirely by their consequences:

What matters is welfare — happiness, well-being, the absence of suffering
All individuals count equally; no one gets extra weight
The morally required action is the one that maximizes total welfare

Bentham called this the hedonic calculus — weigh the pleasures and pains produced, add them up, choose the highest score (Bentham 1789).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Utilitarianism and the Four Principles
Beneficence is central — acting to maximize welfare just is acting beneficently.
Justice emerges from impartiality: each person counts as one, so unfair distributions that reduce total welfare are wrong.
Autonomy is valued instrumentally — respecting autonomy usually maximizes welfare — but can be overridden when it doesn’t.
Non-Maleficence is captured under the harm side of the ledger.






Note


The tension. A strict utilitarian could justify deceiving a patient if the deception produces better outcomes. Most clinicians resist this — which is why other theories matter.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Thought Question

A city has 10 ventilators and 15 patients who need one to survive. Five of the fifteen have a much higher probability of surviving with ventilator support.
A utilitarian protocol would allocate ventilators to maximize lives saved — which likely means the five highest-probability patients get priority.

Is that fair to the other ten patients?
Does it matter why some patients have lower survival odds — age, disability, underlying illness?
What would the principle of justice say that utilitarianism might miss?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


The utilitarian reply. A sophisticated utilitarian can say: the protocol maximizes welfare across the group, which is what justice requires. The critique is that it treats some lives as worth less, and that the reasons behind lower survival odds (disability, chronic illness) are often tied to existing inequities. We will return to this in L9 (Public Health Ethics) and L8 (Justice).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Kant




Immanuel Kant, painted portrait. Public domain (Wikimedia Commons).


Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) argued that morality is grounded in reason, not consequences.
The key test is his Categorical Imperative — a rule for whether an action is morally permissible, applied before you look at outcomes.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
The Humanity Formulation
The most clinically relevant form of the Categorical Imperative (Kant 1785):

“Act so that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in that of another, always as an end and never as a means only.”

In practice, this means:

Persons have dignity — an unconditional worth that cannot be traded off
Deceiving, manipulating, or coercing a patient uses them as a mere means
Informed consent is not just policy — it is a moral requirement

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: Kantian Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
Kantian Ethics and the Four Principles
Autonomy is Kant’s central principle — respecting autonomy is what it means to treat persons as ends.
Non-Maleficence reflects the duty not to harm persons who have inherent dignity.
Beneficence is a genuine duty, but cannot override autonomy — benefiting someone by deceiving them is still wrong.
Justice flows from treating persons equally as ends — no one’s dignity is worth more than another’s.






Note


Contrast with utilitarianism. Kant would say: you must never lie to the Jehovah’s Witness patient about her prognosis to get her to consent, even if lying would save her life.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Thought Question

A patient with severe depression has been refusing an antidepressant. His psychiatrist suspects the refusal is itself a symptom of the illness — that his depression is preventing him from appreciating how treatment could help him.

Does the Kantian view help here — or complicate it?
When, if ever, does treating the illness require overriding the patient’s expressed wishes?
How is this different from the Jehovah’s Witness case?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


The Kantian tension. Kant’s framework requires that the patient be capable of rational self-governance for autonomy to apply fully. If the illness itself impairs that capacity, overriding expressed wishes may be consistent with treating him as an end — but this reasoning can also be abused to justify paternalism. Decision-making capacity assessment (L2) is how clinical ethics tries to hold this line carefully.

text:
Aristotle and Eudaimonia




Aristotle (copy of Lysippos bust). Palazzo Altemps. Public domain.


Aristotle asked not “what should I do?” but “what kind of person should I be?” (Aristotle 340 BC)
Eudaimonia — often translated flourishing — is the goal of human life: living and acting in accordance with the virtues of a fully developed human being.
A virtue is a stable character trait — developed through practice — that reliably disposes a person to act and feel well.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: The Virtues of the Good Nurse
text:
The Virtues of the Good Nurse
Virtue ethics is especially useful in professional ethics, because professions define role-specific excellences.
The ANA Code of Ethics (American Nurses Association 2015) describes the good nurse as someone who embodies:

Compassion — genuine concern for the patient’s suffering
Integrity — consistency between values and actions, even under pressure
Fidelity — keeping commitments made to patients and profession
Practical wisdom (phronesis) — knowing how to apply general principles to particular situations

These are not policies. They are traits you develop by practicing them.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: Virtue Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
Virtue Ethics and the Four Principles
Virtue ethics asks: what would a person of good character do in this situation?
Beneficence — the virtuous clinician genuinely cares about the patient’s good, not just follows protocols.
Non-Maleficence — integrity means honesty about what you can and cannot do.
Autonomy — respect for persons flows from compassion and fidelity, not just rules.
Justice — fairness requires the virtue of impartiality even toward patients you find difficult.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Wrapping Up Lecture 1

Bioethics uses a four-principle framework as its shared vocabulary, supported by four major ethical theories that offer different answers to the question of how to weigh those principles. Lecture 2 turns to the history that gave these principles their urgency, and the clinical relationship where they are daily tested.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
In Part A we asked: what are the four principles, and how do you read a moral argument?
Part B asks the next question:

Where do the principles come from — and how do we weigh them when they conflict?

That is where ethical theory comes in.

By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

State the core commitment of utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, and care ethics
Explain how each theory grounds or prioritizes the four principles differently
Apply at least two theories to the same clinical case and show they yield different answers

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
1.1 What Theories Do
The four principles — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice — give us a shared vocabulary. But they do not, by themselves, tell us which principle to prioritize when they conflict (Beauchamp and Childress 2019).
Ethical theories fill that gap. Think of them as lenses:

Each focuses on something real
Each has blind spots
No single lens sees everything

We will use all four throughout the course — not to pick a winner, but to see more of the problem.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.1 Mill and Bentham




J. S. Mill, c. 1870. London Stereoscopic Co. Public domain.




Jeremy Bentham, by H. W. Pickersgill. Public domain.




“Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness; wrong as they tend to produce the reverse of happiness.” — Mill (Mill 1863)

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.2 The Greatest Happiness Principle
Utilitarianism judges actions entirely by their consequences:

What matters is welfare — happiness, well-being, the absence of suffering
All individuals count equally; no one gets extra weight
The morally required action is the one that maximizes total welfare

Bentham called this the hedonic calculus — weigh the pleasures and pains produced, add them up, choose the highest score (Bentham 1789).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.3 Utilitarianism and the Four Principles
Beneficence is central — acting to maximize welfare just is acting beneficently.
Justice emerges from impartiality: each person counts as one, so unfair distributions that reduce total welfare are wrong.
Autonomy is valued instrumentally — respecting autonomy usually maximizes welfare — but can be overridden when it doesn’t.
Non-Maleficence is captured under the harm side of the ledger.






Note



The tension. A strict utilitarian could justify deceiving a patient if the deception produces better outcomes. Most clinicians resist this — which is why other theories matter.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.5 Thought Question

A city has 10 ventilators and 15 patients who need one to survive. Five of the fifteen have a much higher probability of surviving with ventilator support.
A utilitarian protocol would allocate ventilators to maximize lives saved — which likely means the five highest-probability patients get priority.

Is that fair to the other ten patients?
Does it matter why some patients have lower survival odds — age, disability, underlying illness?
What would the principle of justice say that utilitarianism might miss?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



The utilitarian reply. A sophisticated utilitarian can say: the protocol maximizes welfare across the group, which is what justice requires. The critique is that it treats some lives as worth less, and that the reasons behind lower survival odds (disability, chronic illness) are often tied to existing inequities. We will return to this in L9 (Public Health Ethics) and L8 (Justice).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.1 Kant




Immanuel Kant, painted portrait. Public domain (Wikimedia Commons).


Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) argued that morality is grounded in reason, not consequences.
The key test is his Categorical Imperative — a rule for whether an action is morally permissible, applied before you look at outcomes.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.2 The Humanity Formulation
The most clinically relevant form of the Categorical Imperative (Kant 1785):

“Act so that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in that of another, always as an end and never as a means only.”

In practice, this means:

Persons have dignity — an unconditional worth that cannot be traded off
Deceiving, manipulating, or coercing a patient uses them as a mere means
Informed consent is not just policy — it is a moral requirement

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 3.3 Kantian Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
3.3 Kantian Ethics and the Four Principles
Autonomy is Kant’s central principle — respecting autonomy is what it means to treat persons as ends.
Non-Maleficence reflects the duty not to harm persons who have inherent dignity.
Beneficence is a genuine duty, but cannot override autonomy — benefiting someone by deceiving them is still wrong.
Justice flows from treating persons equally as ends — no one’s dignity is worth more than another’s.






Note



Contrast with utilitarianism. Kant would say: you must never lie to the Jehovah’s Witness patient about her prognosis to get her to consent, even if lying would save her life.

A patient with severe depression has been refusing an antidepressant. His psychiatrist suspects the refusal is itself a symptom of the illness — that his depression is preventing him from appreciating how treatment could help him.
